intro: Hey, welcome back to The Weekly Rundown — grab a snack, because this week did NOT hold back. From Monaco to Madison Square Garden, courtrooms to clay courts, we've got drama, upsets, and a retirement or two to break down together.

stories.headline: Herbstreit Out of EA College Football 27, But He's Got a Book Deal to Cry Into
stories.body: Kirk Herbstreit won't be voicing your dynasty mode this year — EA Sports College Football 27 is moving on without him. But Herbie's staying busy, announcing a new book about his life in football, family, and broadcasting. Honestly, a book feels very on-brand for a guy who has never once been short of words.

stories.headline: Kyler Murray Is Running the Show in Minnesota, and Vikings Fans Are Cautiously Excited
stories.body: Through OTAs, Kyler Murray has emerged as the clear frontrunner at quarterback for the Vikings, and apparently what he's doing in practice is turning heads. It's early, obviously — OTAs basically mean nothing until they mean everything — but the buzz around Murray in a new system is real. Vikings fans have been burned before, so let's call this 'optimistic nervousness.'

stories.headline: Eric Weddle's 2022 Comeback Still Hurts — Literally — and He Wants Aaron Donald to Know
stories.body: Eric Weddle came out of retirement for the Rams' 2022 playoff run, helped them win, and apparently his body has been sending him the bill ever since. The former safety says he's still physically feeling the effects years later, and he's got some words of wisdom for Aaron Donald if he's thinking about doing something similar. The message seems to be: the glory is real, but so is the physical cost.

stories.headline: The Knicks Are Hosting the Finals and Spike Lee Already Won
stories.body: Game 3 of the NBA Finals heads to Madison Square Garden on June 8, with the Knicks looking to build on their series against the Spurs. And before tip-off even happens, Spike Lee is already stealing the show — he's set to pull up to MSG in a custom Air Jordan 3 PE, because of course he is. Some things in this league are just constants.

stories.headline: Vucevic's Boston Experiment Is Over, and Nobody's Shocked
stories.body: Nikola Vucevic is reportedly a near-certainty to leave the Celtics this offseason, which is one of those moves that feels like a surprise to absolutely no one. His stint in Boston was pretty rough, and the fit never really clicked the way the team probably hoped. Onwards.

stories.headline: Knicks vs. Spurs, Game 3: Everything You Need to Know Before Sunday
stories.body: With the series shifting to New York, oddsmakers and analysts are digging into the numbers ahead of Game 3 on June 8. The Spurs have been a fun story all postseason, but playing at MSG with the whole city buzzing is a different animal entirely. Should be a great one.

stories.headline: The Tigers Trade Rumor Mill Is Spinning, and the Cubs Want In
stories.body: ESPN floated a Cubs-Tigers deal centered around ace Tarik Skubal, and apparently it's not the worst idea Detroit has heard. Skubal is one of the best pitchers in baseball, so any trade package better be eye-watering — but it's worth watching as the deadline creeps closer.

stories.headline: The White Sox Might Be Done With Their $8M Reliever Already
stories.body: Jordan Hicks has not been good for Chicago this season, and the word is he could get DFA'd the moment he comes off the IL. For a team already having a historically rough year, it's just another rough chapter in a very long book.

stories.headline: Soroka Saves the D-backs From an Ugly Sweep
stories.body: Michael Soroka turned in another quiet gem on Sunday, leading Arizona to a 5-1 win over Washington to salvage at least one game of the homestand. Soroka has been a low-key pleasant story this season — nobody's talking about him, and he seems totally fine with that.

stories.headline: Bettor Luck Next Time, NCAA — Sorsby Wins His Court Battle
stories.body: Texas Tech QB Brendan Sorsby took the NCAA to court over his gambling-related ineligibility ruling, and a judge basically told the NCAA to pump the brakes. He'll sit two games, but he's cleared to play in 2026 — which is a way better outcome than the full ban the NCAA was pushing for. The NCAA's grip on eligibility decisions keeps getting loosened in court, and honestly, this is becoming a pattern.

stories.headline: Texas vs. Oregon in the College Baseball Super Regionals Is Must-Watch TV
stories.body: The NCAA Baseball Super Regionals are heating up, and Texas hosting Oregon at UFCU Disch-Falk Field on ESPN is the marquee matchup of the weekend. The Longhorns playing at home in Austin gives them a serious edge, but Oregon isn't making the trip just to sightsee. Grab the remote — Karl Ravech and the crew will have you covered starting at 9 ET.

stories.headline: AJ Ferrari Wants to Finish His College Career Alongside His Brother — That's Actually Really Sweet
stories.body: Former NCAA wrestling champion AJ Ferrari has entered the transfer portal for his final collegiate season, and he's dropping a twist: he wants to pick whatever school his brother chooses. It's a feel-good wrinkle in what's usually a pretty transactional portal process. Whichever program lands both of them is going to have a very fun recruiting story to tell.

stories.headline: Not Again: Eriksen Collapses on the Pitch — But He's Okay
stories.body: Christian Eriksen gave everyone a serious case of déjà vu on Sunday when he collapsed during Denmark's friendly against Ukraine — just five years after his terrifying cardiac arrest at Euro 2020. The good news: he was conscious and reportedly doing okay shortly after. Honestly, the guy has been through more on a soccer field than most people face in a lifetime, and his resilience never stops being remarkable.

stories.headline: World Cup vs. Olympics: Which One Actually Moves the Needle?
stories.body: With Utah gearing up to host the 2034 Winter Olympics, folks out there are already debating whether the World Cup — also headed to the U.S. — carries more cultural and economic weight. It's a genuinely interesting question, and depending on who you ask, the answer changes fast. Soccer fans will tell you there's nothing bigger than the World Cup; everyone else might beg to differ.

stories.headline: Antonelli Conquers Monaco — The Kid Is For Real
stories.body: Kimi Antonelli rolled into Monte Carlo already sitting pretty at the top of the drivers' championship, and he didn't let the pressure get to him on one of the most unforgiving tracks on the calendar. Winning at Monaco is a statement — it's narrow, it's chaotic, and it eats young drivers alive. Apparently nobody told Antonelli that.

stories.headline: Antonelli Tightens His Grip On The 2026 Title Race
stories.body: With the Monaco result in the books, Antonelli is putting some real daylight between himself and the rest of the field in the standings. We're not even at the halfway point of the season, but the way he's been driving, rivals are going to need to find something extra — and fast. The championship picture is starting to look a lot less like a question mark.

stories.headline: NC State Alum Shnaider Knocks Out Sabalenka at the French Open
stories.body: Diana Shnaider pulled off one of the bigger upsets of the clay season, taking down world number one Aryna Sabalenka at Roland Garros. The Russian, who honed her game at NC State, is now chasing history — no former college player has won a women's Grand Slam singles title since 1979, which feels like a fun trivia answer waiting to happen.

stories.headline: Off-Court Controversy Follows Shnaider Into the Spotlight
stories.body: With the big win comes the big scrutiny. Ukrainian player Oleksandra Oliynykova is publicly calling for sanctions against Shnaider, alleging ties to Gazprom — the Russian state energy company — through a sponsored event. It's a reminder that tennis, especially right now, doesn't exist in a vacuum, and Shnaider's rising profile means these questions are only going to get louder.

stories.headline: Don't Miss the French Open Men's Final This Weekend
stories.body: The men's clay court crown is up for grabs, with the French Open Men's Final set for Sunday, June 8th. Clear your schedule, grab something to eat, and settle in — Roland Garros finals have a way of delivering.

stories.headline: Milan Lucic Hangs Up the Skates After 17 Hard-Nosed Seasons
stories.body: Milan Lucic has officially called it a career, closing the book on one of the more physically imposing runs in recent NHL history. The man won a Cup with Boston in 2011 and spent nearly two decades making life miserable for opposing defensemen. His wife Brittany shared some heartfelt tributes online, and honestly the whole thing feels like a proper sendoff for a guy who always played the game the hard way.

stories.headline: Dylan Larkin Wants Out of Detroit — And Teams Are Lining Up
stories.body: Larkin officially requested a trade from the Red Wings last week, and apparently three teams are already circling. This is a big deal — he's been the face of Detroit's rebuild, so if he's ready to move on, it says something about where that franchise is at. Expect this one to get louder as the offseason heats up.

stories.headline: Colorado Avalanche Exit the WCF — What Went Wrong?
stories.body: The Avs are heading home after falling in the Western Conference Final, and people are already doing the post-mortem. For a team with that much talent, bowing out at this stage stings, and questions about goaltending — including over in Carolina — are swirling around the league right now. It's the kind of early exit that fuels a very long summer of what-ifs.

stories.headline: Nelly Korda Wins the US Women's Open While Barely Hitting a Green — Somehow
stories.body: Korda missed 30 greens in regulation at Riviera and still walked away with the US Women's Open trophy, which is either the most impressive or most chaotic win in recent memory — probably both. She's now staking a serious claim as the defining face of American women's golf for a generation. Short game coach of the year award goes to whoever is in her corner right now.

stories.headline: Six Golfers Miss a Major Qualifier Because of a Medical Emergency at 30,000 Feet
stories.body: A group of PGA Tour Americas players were on a flight from Mexico when a medical emergency forced a diversion, causing them to miss the final US Open qualifier — a brutal break that was entirely out of their hands. One player, Chris Nido, somehow made it to the tee box but had to play with a friend's clubs after leaving his own behind in Mexico. That's the kind of week that makes you question your life choices.

stories.headline: A CBS Sports Crew Member Died This Week, and the Golf World Is Grieving
stories.body: Bryce Adair, a 31-year-old CBS Sports production assistant, passed away following a car crash while he was working a golf tournament. Jim Nantz said it simply: 'Everyone loved him.' Worth pausing the highlights reel for a moment to acknowledge the people who make the broadcasts we enjoy every week possible.
